segments.title: Shadow fleet protection risks open escalation
segments.question: Are the Russians likely to reactivate Kuznetsov and stop scrapping it to protect the shadow fleet?
segments.answerShort: No. Dr. Clarke argues that physically shielding shadow-fleet ships would quickly run into escalation choices, because stopping boarding aircraft or boats would risk an act of war.
segments.summary: A Bruships answer rejects reactivating Kuznetsov for shadow-fleet protection and frames the problem as escalation rather than simple escort presence.
segments.body: This Q&A captures a concise current-affairs answer from the live stream. Dr. Clarke says Russia is unlikely to reactivate Kuznetsov for shadow-fleet protection because a warship can only obstruct inspections so far before facing escalation choices over helicopters, boats, and use of force. He then widens the point into skepticism about taking defense-sales claims at face value.

evidence.note: The audience asks about reactivating Kuznetsov to protect the shadow fleet, and the answer says no while explaining that physically stopping warships leads toward war-risk decisions.
The answer frames shadow-fleet protection as shadow boxing, with Russia unlikely to want open war over the fleet while already occupied in Ukraine.
The follow-on caveat warns against treating Russian defense claims as inherently more reliable than Western arms-sales claims.
